comments.text: In general, doubly exponential sequences $a^{b^n}+c$ can be shown to have composite values, If one excludes the case where they form a subsequence of Fermat numbers.

I was able to fully close the $3$ main questions after exploring a few ideas with GPT Pro (with the exception for the added always squarefree, infinitely often a prime and infinitely often squarefree questions). TEX here PDF here LEAN here My guess would be that the squarefree questions are at their core the question of whether there all Fermat numbers are squarefree, which is a famous open problem. The infinitely often prime question seems to be comparable to the question of whether Fermat primes are infinite. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks! I ran standard check which found no issues. For questions 1 and 2, a similar construction to the one in the problem description is provided which blocks both primes and squarefree numbers at once. For question 3, we reason about multiplicative order to get some $2^{2^{t+L}} \equiv 2^{2^t} \pmod p$ if $p = n + 2^{2^t}$ is prime.
Thanks - although the first two questions are already answered by the trivial construction given in the problem remarks.

Yes, but the trivial construction is only assuming one only wants a counterexample for positive shifts. I strengthened this by using a sequence for which $n=0$ is the uinque shift for which all $n+a_k$ are prime (and sqarefree), while still allowing arbitrarily fast growth.

As usual in number theoretic problems by 'integers' we generally mean only positive integers. Regardless, the construction in the remarks can be trivially altered to allow for negative $n$ as well; this modification is what is done in your note.
